content.text: Why Claude Incidents Need a Purpose-Built Severity Matrix

Generic severity matrices designed for web services do not translate directly to Claude API integrations. Claude incidents have characteristics that require explicit classification criteria: non-deterministic outputs (a degraded model may produce wrong answers rather than errors), token-level billing impact, rate limit effects that create cascading failures, and context window exhaustion patterns that are invisible to standard HTTP error monitoring.

A purpose-built severity matrix answers two operational questions instantly: how urgently must the on-call respond, and who needs to be notified beyond the primary on-call. The matrix below defines four severity levels (P0–P3) with explicit, measurable classification criteria for Claude-specific failure modes.

The cost of misclassifying an incident is asymmetric: treating a P1 as a P2 delays the response by 3.5 hours. Treating a P3 as a P1 burns the on-call and erodes the team's sense of urgency. The matrix must be unambiguous.

attrs.alt: Severity matrix: blast radius vs impact confidence for Claude production incidents

content.text: Each severity level is defined by three orthogonal dimensions: blast radius (percentage of production traffic or users affected), impact confidence (how certain we are that something is broken), and business impact (revenue or SLA consequences). All three must be considered — a confident detection of a minor bug is not a P0.

Complete or near-complete loss of service. Affects ≥50% of production traffic or all traffic in a critical region. Anthropic status page shows an incident, or your own monitoring confirms >50% error rate across the Claude API.
Claude API returns 5xx for >50% of requests across all endpoint types.
Authentication failures for all API keys (not a single key — the entire key space).
Response latency exceeds 120 seconds for >50% of requests (timeout at infrastructure level).
Billing or quota system offline: no requests are accepted regardless of load.
Streaming responses return corrupted data (truncated or garbled output) for >50% of calls.
Significant service degradation affecting a substantial portion of users or a critical subset of functionality. Not a complete outage, but measurably breaking user-facing behavior in production.
API error rate between 5% and 50% across production traffic.
P99 latency exceeds 30 seconds for >10% of requests (interactive applications become unusable).
A specific model version (e.g., claude-opus-4-8) is returning consistently degraded outputs — confirmed by automated quality probes, not just user reports.
Rate limit responses (429) spike to >20% of traffic, indicating quota exhaustion or rate limiter bug.
Token counting returns values outside the known tolerance band for a specific request pattern — financial exposure from billing errors.
Streaming responses stall mid-response for >10% of requests (SSE connection drops).
Localized degradation or degradation affecting a small percentage of traffic. Service is operational but with measurable quality or performance reduction that will affect some users.
API error rate between 1% and 5%, or elevated in a single region only.
P95 latency exceeds 10 seconds but P99 is under 30 seconds.
A specific API feature (e.g., function calling, vision inputs) shows elevated error rates while the core text completion endpoint is healthy.
Model output quality probe detects regression below threshold for a non-critical endpoint.
Single customer account experiencing systematic failures (not a global issue).
Notify team lead if no ack in 30 min
Minor issues with negligible user impact. Typically discovered in dashboards or automated probes rather than by users or customer reports. No immediate action required outside business hours.
Error rate below 1% with no user-visible behavior change (noise floor).
Latency elevated by 20–30% over baseline but still within SLO.
Single-request failures that resolve on retry without pattern.
Alerts firing at borderline thresholds without confirmed user impact.
Standard HTTP metrics (error rate, latency) are necessary but not sufficient for Claude. The following Claude-specific failure modes each have defined classification rules.

A degraded Claude model may return HTTP 200 responses with syntactically valid JSON but semantically incorrect outputs. This is the hardest failure mode to classify — it requires automated quality probes rather than just error rate monitoring.
Investigate model version; check Anthropic status
Treat as degraded model; consider rollback to prior version
Probe returns completely wrong output class

Rate limit responses (HTTP 429) are expected in normal operation when individual clients burst above their tier. They become incidents only when the rate of 429 responses across the fleet indicates a system-wide quota problem or a rate limiter bug.
Quota exhaustion or misconfigured limits; investigate
Rate limiter bug or global quota exhaustion
Context Window and Token Counting Failures
Context window overflow (HTTP 400 with context_length_exceeded) and token counting anomalies are classification-specific to LLM APIs. These require context-aware triage.
Single-request context length exceeded: P3, not an incident. The application is sending oversized payloads.
Systematic context length errors for a request pattern that was previously working: P2. The model's context window may have changed, or token counting changed.
Token count returned by the API diverges from the tiktoken estimate by more than 10%: P2 (billing impact). Investigate token counter service.
Token count returns 0 or negative values: P1. Token counter is broken; all billing calculations are invalid.

The decision tree below provides an unambiguous path from observed symptom to severity level. On-call engineers should follow this tree before assigning severity — personal judgment of impact is not sufficient without checking the criteria.

# severity_classifier.py — programmatic severity classification for Claude incidents
from enum import Enum

class Severity(str, Enum):
    P0 = "P0"
    P1 = "P1"
    P2 = "P2"
    P3 = "P3"

def classify_incident(
    error_rate: float,        # 0.0–1.0
    p99_latency_sec: float,
    affected_traffic_pct: float,  # 0.0–1.0
    quality_drop_pct: float,  # 0.0–1.0 (0 = no quality drop)
    rate_429_pct: float,      # 0.0–1.0
) -> Severity:
    """Return the severity level for a Claude production incident."""

    # P0 checks
    if error_rate > 0.50:
        return Severity.P0
    if affected_traffic_pct > 0.50 and error_rate > 0.10:
        return Severity.P0
    if quality_drop_pct > 0.15:
        return Severity.P0

    # P1 checks
    if error_rate > 0.05:
        return Severity.P1
    if p99_latency_sec > 30 and affected_traffic_pct > 0.10:
        return Severity.P1
    if rate_429_pct > 0.15:
        return Severity.P1
    if quality_drop_pct > 0.05:
        return Severity.P1

    # P2 checks
    if error_rate > 0.01:
        return Severity.P2
    if p99_latency_sec > 10:
        return Severity.P2
    if rate_429_pct > 0.05:
        return Severity.P2
    if quality_drop_pct > 0:
        return Severity.P2

    # Default: P3
    return Severity.P3

# Example: 8% error rate, P99=12s, affecting 20% of traffic
sev = classify_incident(
    error_rate=0.08,
    p99_latency_sec=12.0,
    affected_traffic_pct=0.20,
    quality_drop_pct=0.0,
    rate_429_pct=0.02,
)
print(f"Classified as: {sev}")  # P1

Initial severity classification is a best-effort assessment under time pressure. Severity can and should change as more information becomes available. There are explicit rules for upgrades and downgrades to prevent the matrix from being gamed in either direction.
Any incident that has not shown improvement in the first half of its resolution SLA is automatically upgraded by one level (P2 → P1 after 4 hours with no progress).
Any incident where customer-reported complaints exceed 10 unique customers is upgraded to at least P2.
Any incident where Anthropic's own status page confirms an active incident is upgraded to match the reported severity.
P2 incidents affecting financial calculations (billing, token counts) are upgraded to P1 regardless of traffic percentage.
Severity can only be downgraded by the incident commander, not the primary on-call, and only after the following conditions are met:
Metrics have returned to within 10% of baseline for at least 15 consecutive minutes.
Root cause is identified and confirmed (not suspected).
Mitigation is in place (not just planned).
No new customer complaints have been received in the last 30 minutes.
Downgrading from P0 to P1 does not end the incident — it ends the war room. The primary on-call continues to own the incident until full resolution and a post-incident review is scheduled.
Prometheus Alerting Rules for Automatic Classification

The severity matrix should be wired into your Prometheus alerting rules so that alerts fire with the correct severity label. This prevents on-call engineers from manually classifying every alert and ensures consistent routing to the right escalation path.

# prometheus-alerts-claude-severity.yml
groups:
  - name: claude_severity_classification
    rules:
      # P0: >50% error rate
      - alert: ClaudeAPIErrorRateP0
        expr: |
          sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total[5m]))
            / sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m])) > 0.50
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: P0
          team: sre
        annotations:
          summary: "P0: Claude API error rate >50% ({{ $value | humanizePercentage }})"
          runbook: "https://runbooks.internal/claude/p0-api-outage"

      # P1: 5–50% error rate
      - alert: ClaudeAPIErrorRateP1
        expr: |
          sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total[5m]))
            / sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m])) > 0.05
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: P1
          team: sre
        annotations:
          summary: "P1: Claude API error rate elevated ({{ $value | humanizePercentage }})"
          runbook: "https://runbooks.internal/claude/p1-error-rate"

      # P1: P99 latency >30s
      - alert: ClaudeAPILatencyP1
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.99,
            sum(rate(claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)
          ) > 30
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: P1
          team: sre
        annotations:
          summary: "P1: Claude API P99 latency {{ $value | humanizeDuration }} > 30s"
          runbook: "https://runbooks.internal/claude/p1-latency"

      # P2: 1–5% error rate
      - alert: ClaudeAPIErrorRateP2
        expr: |
          sum(rate(claude_api_errors_total[5m]))
            / sum(rate(claude_api_requests_total[5m])) > 0.01
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: P2
          team: sre
        annotations:
          summary: "P2: Claude API error rate slightly elevated ({{ $value | humanizePercentage }})"
          runbook: "https://runbooks.internal/claude/p2-error-rate"

A purpose-built severity matrix for Claude incidents classifies on four dimensions: error rate, latency, blast radius, and Claude-specific signals (quality probes, rate limit rate, token counting anomalies). The four levels — P0 (complete outage, 5-minute SLA), P1 (significant degradation, 30-minute SLA), P2 (localized degradation, 4-hour SLA), P3 (minor issues, next business day) — each carry explicit response requirements and escalation triggers.
P0: ≥50% error rate or complete API outage; 5-min ack SLA; war room in 10 min; executive notification.
P1: 5–50% error rate or P99 >30s; 10-min ack SLA; mitigation started within 30 min.
P2: 1–5% error rate or localized degradation; 30-min ack SLA; 8-hour resolution SLA.
P3: <1% error rate, no user-visible impact; next business day.
Quality probes must supplement HTTP error monitoring — a degraded model returns 200 but breaks applications.
Wire severity classification into Prometheus alerting labels to ensure consistent routing without manual classification.
Severity upgrades are automatic (no improvement at half the resolution SLA); downgrades require incident commander sign-off.
